# Nonlinear Relationships {#sec-ch6} ## Introduction {#sec-ch6-intro} The linearity assumption is one of the most important --- and most frequently violated --- assumptions in regression. Many relationships in education research are not linear: the effect of an additional year of schooling may diminish at higher levels, test score gains may plateau, and the relationship between socioeconomic status and achievement often shows curvature. This chapter introduces several tools for modeling nonlinear relationships within the linear regression framework: LOWESS curves for detection, log transformations, polynomial regression, and piecewise models. Crucially, all of these approaches remain "linear" models --- they are linear in the *parameters*, even when the relationship between $x$ and $y$ is curved. ::: {.objectives} [Learning Objectives]{.obj-title} After completing this chapter, you will be able to: 1. Use LOWESS curves to detect nonlinearity in bivariate relationships 2. Apply and interpret log transformations (log-level, level-log, log-log) 3. Fit and interpret polynomial regression models (quadratic, cubic) 4. Understand piecewise constant models as ANOVA on a discretized predictor 5. Choose among approaches based on theory and data characteristics ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Connection to Prior Chapters This chapter extends the linear regression framework from Chapters 1--4 by **relaxing the linearity assumption**. Key connections include: - **Residual diagnostics** (@sec-ch2): Nonlinearity often appears as a curved pattern in residual-vs-fitted plots --- the techniques here address that violation. - **Nested F-tests** (@sec-ch4): The same `anova()` approach used to compare nested models in Chapter 4 is used here to test whether polynomial terms significantly improve model fit. - **Group-varying effects** (@sec-ch5): Chapter 5 showed that the effect of a predictor can differ across *groups* (interactions). Chapter 6 shows that the effect can also vary across the *range of $x$* itself --- a quadratic means the slope changes continuously as $x$ changes. - All models in this chapter remain **linear in the parameters**, so everything you learned about estimation, inference, and diagnostics still applies. ::: ## Detecting Nonlinearity with LOWESS {#sec-ch6-lowess} **LOWESS** (LOcally WEighted Scatterplot Smoothing) fits a smooth curve through data without assuming a specific functional form. It serves as a diagnostic tool: if the LOWESS curve deviates substantially from a straight line, the linearity assumption may be violated. ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} data(gcse) ggplot(gcse, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.1, color = "grey50") + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], se = FALSE, linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") + labs( title = "Linear Fit vs LOWESS Curve", subtitle = "Blue = linear | Red dashed = LOWESS", x = "LRT Score", y = "GCSE Score" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Comparing a linear fit (blue) with a LOWESS curve (red dashed) for the GCSE-LRT relationship. The two curves nearly overlap, with only a slight deviation at the low-LRT extreme.](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/fig-ch6-lowess-1.png){#fig-ch6-lowess fig-alt='Scatter plot with LRT score on the x-axis and GCSE score on the y-axis. A straight blue regression line and a red dashed LOWESS curve are overlaid on the semi-transparent data points. The LOWESS curve closely follows the linear fit across almost the entire range of LRT, deviating only slightly at the low-LRT extreme, so the curvature is negligible.' width=2400} ::: ::: ### Output Interpretation ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-1-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: Look for where the red dashed LOWESS curve deviates from the blue straight line. In the GCSE-LRT data, the two curves nearly overlap everywhere, with only a slight deviation at the low-LRT extreme: the curvature is negligible and a linear model is adequate here. If the two curves nearly overlap everywhere, a linear model is adequate. If the LOWESS diverges noticeably, consider the nonlinear modeling approaches covered in the rest of this chapter (log transformations, polynomials, or piecewise models). ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} data(gcse) ggplot(gcse, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.1, color = "grey50") + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], se = FALSE, linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") + labs( title = "Linear Fit vs LOWESS Curve", subtitle = "Blue = linear | Red dashed = LOWESS", x = "LRT Score", y = "GCSE Score" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: - `geom_smooth(method = "lm")`: Fits a straight line (assumes linearity) - `geom_smooth(method = "loess")`: Fits a LOWESS curve (no linearity assumption) - If the two curves overlap closely, the linear model is adequate - Divergence between curves (especially at the extremes) suggests nonlinearity ::: ## Log Transformations {#sec-ch6-log} The most common transformation for addressing nonlinearity is the **logarithmic transformation**. There are four log-linear model specifications: | Model | Equation | Interpretation of $\beta$ | |:------|:---------|:--------------------------| | Level-level | $y = \alpha + \beta x$ | 1-unit increase in $x$ $\rightarrow$ $\beta$-unit change in $y$ | | Log-level | $\log(y) = \alpha + \beta x$ | 1-unit increase in $x$ $\rightarrow$ approximately $100\beta$% change in $y$ | | Level-log | $y = \alpha + \beta \log(x)$ | 1% increase in $x$ $\rightarrow$ approximately $\beta/100$ unit change in $y$ | | Log-log | $\log(y) = \alpha + \beta \log(x)$ | 1% increase in $x$ $\rightarrow$ approximately $\beta$% change in $y$ (elasticity) | ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} # Using the crime data: crime rate vs urbanization data(crime) crime_df <- crime |> rename(crime_rate = c, high_school = hs, urbanization = u) |> filter(crime_rate > 0, urbanization > 0) # All four log-linear model specifications m_ll <- lm(crime_rate ~ urbanization, data = crime_df) # Level-level m_loglevel <- lm(log(crime_rate) ~ urbanization, data = crime_df) # Log-level m_levellog <- lm(crime_rate ~ log(urbanization), data = crime_df) # Level-log m_loglog <- lm(log(crime_rate) ~ log(urbanization), data = crime_df) # Log-log ``` ::: ### Output Interpretation ::: {.cell} ::: Compare the four models: - **Level-level**: The urbanization coefficient tells you the expected change in crime rate (in raw units) per percentage point increase in urbanization. - **Log-level**: Multiply the coefficient by 100 to get the approximate percentage change in crime rate per unit increase in urbanization. - **Level-log**: Divide the coefficient by 100 to get the approximate unit change in crime rate per 1% increase in urbanization. - **Log-log**: The coefficient is the elasticity --- a 1% increase in urbanization is associated with approximately a $\beta$% change in crime rate. Compare $R^2$ **only among models that share the same response scale** --- for example, level-level versus level-log (both have $y$ as the outcome), or log-level versus log-log (both have $\log y$). **$R^2$ is not comparable across models with different response transformations**, because models with $y$ and $\log(y)$ outcomes explain variance on different scales (different total sums of squares). To judge fit *across* response transformations, compare instead via the **residual-plot / LOWESS linearization** shown in @fig-ch6-log-transform (how closely the linear and LOWESS curves overlap) or via **back-transformed prediction error on the original scale** (e.g., RMSE computed after exponentiating the fitted log-values). If the log-level or log-log specification linearizes the relationship more cleanly, the multiplicative specification may be more appropriate for these data. ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Using the crime data: crime rate vs urbanization data(crime) crime_df <- crime |> rename(crime_rate = c, high_school = hs, urbanization = u) |> filter(crime_rate > 0, urbanization > 0) # All four log-linear model specifications m_ll <- lm(crime_rate ~ urbanization, data = crime_df) # Level-level m_loglevel <- lm(log(crime_rate) ~ urbanization, data = crime_df) # Log-level m_levellog <- lm(crime_rate ~ log(urbanization), data = crime_df) # Level-log m_loglog <- lm(log(crime_rate) ~ log(urbanization), data = crime_df) # Log-log ``` ::: - **Level-level** (`crime_rate ~ urbanization`): The baseline model with no transformations; the coefficient is in raw units (crimes per 1000 per percentage point of urbanization) - **Log-level** (`log(crime_rate) ~ urbanization`): Log-transforming the outcome estimates the effect in approximate percentage terms - **Level-log** (`crime_rate ~ log(urbanization)`): Log-transforming the predictor captures diminishing returns --- each additional percentage of urbanization has a smaller effect - **Log-log** (`log(crime_rate) ~ log(urbanization)`): Both sides logged; the coefficient is an **elasticity** (% change in $y$ per 1% change in $x$) - Log transformations are appropriate when the relationship shows diminishing returns or when data spans a wide range ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Log Interpretation Rules - $\log(y)$ model: Think in **percentages for $y$**. A coefficient of 0.05 means a ~5% increase in $y$ per unit increase in $x$. - $\log(x)$ model: Think in **percentages for $x$**. A coefficient of $\beta$ means a **$\beta \cdot \ln 2 \approx 0.69\,\beta$ unit change in $y$ per doubling of $x$** (equivalently, $\beta$ units per $e$-fold $\approx 2.718\times$ increase; or, for small changes, $\approx \beta/100$ units per 1% increase in $x$). So a coefficient of 2 implies about a 1.39-unit change in $y$ per doubling of $x$, **not** 2. - $\log$-$\log$ model: $\beta$ is the **elasticity** --- the percentage change in $y$ per 1% change in $x$. ::: ### Visualizing the Log Transformation ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} p_ll <- ggplot(crime_df, aes(x = urbanization, y = crime_rate)) + geom_point(color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], alpha = 0.6) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], se = FALSE) + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed") + labs(title = "Level-Level", x = "Urbanization (%)", y = "Crime Rate") p_loglev <- ggplot(crime_df, aes(x = urbanization, y = log(crime_rate))) + geom_point(color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], alpha = 0.6) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], se = FALSE) + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed") + labs(title = "Log-Level", x = "Urbanization (%)", y = "log(Crime Rate)") p_levlog <- ggplot(crime_df, aes(x = log(urbanization), y = crime_rate)) + geom_point(color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], alpha = 0.6) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], se = FALSE) + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed") + labs(title = "Level-Log", x = "log(Urbanization)", y = "Crime Rate") p_loglog <- ggplot(crime_df, aes(x = log(urbanization), y = log(crime_rate))) + geom_point(color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], alpha = 0.6) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], se = FALSE) + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed") + labs(title = "Log-Log", x = "log(Urbanization)", y = "log(Crime Rate)") (p_ll + p_loglev) / (p_levlog + p_loglog) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Four log-linear model specifications applied to the crime-urbanization relationship. Blue lines are linear fits; red dashed lines are LOWESS curves. Comparing how closely the two curves overlap helps judge which specification best linearizes the relationship.](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/fig-ch6-log-transform-1.png){#fig-ch6-log-transform fig-alt='Four-panel scatter plot grid comparing log-linear model specifications for the crime-urbanization relationship. Each panel applies a different combination of log transformations to the x-axis (urbanization) and y-axis (crime rate). In each panel a blue linear fit and a red dashed LOWESS curve are overlaid on the data. Panels where the blue and red lines overlap closely indicate that the log transformation successfully linearized the relationship.' width=3000} ::: ::: ### Output Interpretation ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=3000} ::: ::: Examine which of the four panels shows the closest overlap between the linear fit (blue) and the LOWESS curve (red dashed). The panel where the two curves nearly coincide suggests the most appropriate specification for these data. For the crime-urbanization relationship, you may find that the log-log specification linearizes the relationship best, meaning the elasticity interpretation is most natural: a 1% increase in urbanization is associated with a roughly constant percentage change in crime rate across the full range. ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p_ll <- ggplot(crime_df, aes(x = urbanization, y = crime_rate)) + geom_point(color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], alpha = 0.6) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], se = FALSE) + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed") + labs(title = "Level-Level", x = "Urbanization (%)", y = "Crime Rate") p_loglev <- ggplot(crime_df, aes(x = urbanization, y = log(crime_rate))) + geom_point(color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], alpha = 0.6) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], se = FALSE) + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed") + labs(title = "Log-Level", x = "Urbanization (%)", y = "log(Crime Rate)") p_levlog <- ggplot(crime_df, aes(x = log(urbanization), y = crime_rate)) + geom_point(color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], alpha = 0.6) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], se = FALSE) + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed") + labs(title = "Level-Log", x = "log(Urbanization)", y = "Crime Rate") p_loglog <- ggplot(crime_df, aes(x = log(urbanization), y = log(crime_rate))) + geom_point(color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], alpha = 0.6) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], se = FALSE) + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed") + labs(title = "Log-Log", x = "log(Urbanization)", y = "log(Crime Rate)") (p_ll + p_loglev) / (p_levlog + p_loglog) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=3000} ::: ::: - **Top-left (Level-Level)**: The raw relationship; curvature in the LOWESS curve indicates nonlinearity - **Top-right (Log-Level)**: Log-transforming the outcome compresses large values, potentially linearizing a concave relationship - **Bottom-left (Level-Log)**: Log-transforming the predictor spreads out small values, useful when diminishing returns are present in $x$ - **Bottom-right (Log-Log)**: Both transformations applied simultaneously; the slope is the elasticity - In each panel, closer overlap between the blue line and red dashed LOWESS curve indicates a better linear approximation ::: ### Interactive Exploration: Log Transformation Explorer {.unnumbered} ::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"} ## Try It: Four Ways to Linearize Use the **Model specification** radio buttons (Level–Level, Log–Level, Level–Log, Log–Log) to see how transforming X, Y, or both affects the scatter plot, the fitted regression line, and how we **interpret** the slope coefficient; start on Level–Level to see the raw curvature, then switch to Log–Level and Log–Log to watch the cloud straighten out. Watch how R² changes as you transform X — but **compare R² only within the same Y scale**: the level-Y models (Level–Level, Level–Log) explain variation in Y, while the log-Y models (Log–Level, Log–Log) explain variation in ln Y, so their R² values are *not* on a common scale and cannot be ranked against each other. We simulate the data from a known exponential process ($Y = e^{1.5 + 0.04X + \varepsilon}$) because demonstrating that one specific transformation linearizes the relationship requires a *known multiplicative truth* — a single real dataset would leave you guessing whether any residual curvature came from the model or from the data. ::: ```{ojs} //| echo: false import { ber640 as logtx_ber640, withAlpha as logtx_withAlpha, themeColors as logtx_themeColors, plotDefaults as logtx_plotDefaults, normalRandom as logtx_normalRandom, seq as logtx_seq, formatCoef as logtx_formatCoef, srSummary as logtx_srSummary, resetControls as logtx_resetControls, onThemeChange as logtx_onThemeChange } from "../ojs/shared.js" ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false logtx_theme = logtx_themeColors() ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false logtx_pill = (label, value, tone = "") => html`${label}${value}` ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false viewof logtx_modelType = Inputs.radio( ["Level–Level", "Log–Level", "Level–Log", "Log–Log"], { value: "Level–Level", label: "Model specification" } ) ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false // Seeded PRNG (mulberry32) function logtx_mulberry32(seed) { return function() { seed |= 0; seed = seed + 0x6D2B79F5 | 0; let t = Math.imul(seed ^ seed >>> 15, 1 | seed); t = t + Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 7, 61 | t) ^ t; return ((t ^ t >>> 14) >>> 0) / 4294967296; } } function logtx_normalSample(rng) { const u1 = rng(), u2 = rng(); return Math.sqrt(-2 * Math.log(u1)) * Math.cos(2 * Math.PI * u2); } // Generate data: exponential DGP logtx_n = 80 logtx_data = { const rngX = logtx_mulberry32(42); const rngE = logtx_mulberry32(43); const data = []; for (let i = 0; i < logtx_n; i++) { const x = 1 + rngX() * 49; const eps = logtx_normalSample(rngE) * 0.3; const y = Math.exp(1.5 + 0.04 * x + eps); data.push({ x, y }); } return data; } ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false // Transform data based on model type logtx_transformed = { const mt = logtx_modelType; return logtx_data.map(d => { const tx = (mt === "Level–Log" || mt === "Log–Log") ? Math.log(d.x) : d.x; const ty = (mt === "Log–Level" || mt === "Log–Log") ? Math.log(d.y) : d.y; return { x: d.x, y: d.y, tx, ty }; }); } // OLS on transformed data logtx_ols = { const data = logtx_transformed; const n = data.length; const meanX = d3.mean(data, d => d.tx); const meanY = d3.mean(data, d => d.ty); const ssXY = d3.sum(data, d => (d.tx - meanX) * (d.ty - meanY)); const ssXX = d3.sum(data, d => (d.tx - meanX) ** 2); const b = ssXY / ssXX; const a = meanY - b * meanX; const predicted = data.map(d => a + b * d.tx); const ssTot = d3.sum(data, d => (d.ty - meanY) ** 2); const ssRes = d3.sum(data.map((d, i) => (d.ty - predicted[i]) ** 2)); const r2 = 1 - ssRes / ssTot; return { a, b, r2 }; } // All 4 R² values logtx_allR2 = { const models = ["Level–Level", "Log–Level", "Level–Log", "Log–Log"]; const results = {}; for (const mt of models) { const trans = logtx_data.map(d => { const tx = (mt === "Level–Log" || mt === "Log–Log") ? Math.log(d.x) : d.x; const ty = (mt === "Log–Level" || mt === "Log–Log") ? Math.log(d.y) : d.y; return { tx, ty }; }); const meanX = d3.mean(trans, d => d.tx); const meanY = d3.mean(trans, d => d.ty); const ssXY = d3.sum(trans, d => (d.tx - meanX) * (d.ty - meanY)); const ssXX = d3.sum(trans, d => (d.tx - meanX) ** 2); const b = ssXY / ssXX; const a = meanY - b * meanX; const ssTot = d3.sum(trans, d => (d.ty - meanY) ** 2); const ssRes = d3.sum(trans, d => (d.ty - (a + b * d.tx)) ** 2); results[mt] = 1 - ssRes / ssTot; } return results; } logtx_bestModel = { // R² is only comparable WITHIN a common Y scale, so pick the best model // SEPARATELY for the level-Y group and the log-Y group. Returning a Set // (one winner per scale) avoids implying a single cross-scale champion. const levelY = ["Level–Level", "Level–Log"]; // R² computed on Y const logY = ["Log–Level", "Log–Log"]; // R² computed on ln(Y) const argmax = group => { let best = null, bestR2 = -Infinity; for (const m of group) { if (logtx_allR2[m] > bestR2) { bestR2 = logtx_allR2[m]; best = m; } } return best; }; return new Set([argmax(levelY), argmax(logY)]); } ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false // LOESS smoother logtx_loessSmooth = { function tricube(u) { const au = Math.abs(u); return au < 1 ? Math.pow(1 - Math.pow(au, 3), 3) : 0; } const data = logtx_transformed; const xs = data.map(d => d.tx), ys = data.map(d => d.ty); const xMin = d3.min(xs), xMax = d3.max(xs); const grid = logtx_seq(xMin, xMax, 80); const h = (xMax - xMin) * 0.3; return grid.map(gx => { let wS=0, wxS=0, wxxS=0, wyS=0, wxyS=0; for (let i = 0; i < xs.length; i++) { const dx = xs[i] - gx; const w = tricube(dx / (h || 1)); wS += w; wxS += w*dx; wxxS += w*dx*dx; wyS += w*ys[i]; wxyS += w*dx*ys[i]; } const denom = wS*wxxS - wxS*wxS; const yhat = denom !== 0 ? (wxxS*wyS - wxS*wxyS) / denom : wyS / (wS || 1); return { tx: gx, ty: yhat }; }); } // Axis labels logtx_xLabel = (logtx_modelType === "Level–Log" || logtx_modelType === "Log–Log") ? "ln(X)" : "X" logtx_yLabel = (logtx_modelType === "Log–Level" || logtx_modelType === "Log–Log") ? "ln(Y)" : "Y" ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false // Interpretation strings (slope meaning per model type) logtx_interp = { const { a, b } = logtx_ols; const mt = logtx_modelType; let equation, meaning; switch (mt) { case "Level–Level": equation = `Y = ${logtx_formatCoef(a, 3)} + ${logtx_formatCoef(b, 4)} · X`; meaning = `A 1-unit ↑ in X → ${logtx_formatCoef(Math.abs(b), 4)}-unit ${b >= 0 ? "↑" : "↓"} in Y.`; break; case "Log–Level": equation = `ln(Y) = ${logtx_formatCoef(a, 3)} + ${logtx_formatCoef(b, 4)} · X`; meaning = `A 1-unit ↑ in X → ${logtx_formatCoef(Math.abs(b * 100), 2)}% ${b >= 0 ? "↑" : "↓"} in Y.`; break; case "Level–Log": equation = `Y = ${logtx_formatCoef(a, 3)} + ${logtx_formatCoef(b, 3)} · ln(X)`; meaning = `A 1% ↑ in X → ${logtx_formatCoef(Math.abs(b / 100), 4)}-unit ${b >= 0 ? "↑" : "↓"} in Y.`; break; case "Log–Log": equation = `ln(Y) = ${logtx_formatCoef(a, 3)} + ${logtx_formatCoef(b, 4)} · ln(X)`; meaning = `A 1% ↑ in X → ${logtx_formatCoef(Math.abs(b), 4)}% ${b >= 0 ? "↑" : "↓"} in Y (elasticity).`; break; } return { equation, meaning }; } ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false logtx_readouts = html`
${logtx_pill("Slope b", logtx_formatCoef(logtx_ols.b, 4), "info")} ${logtx_pill("R² (" + logtx_yLabel + " scale)", logtx_ols.r2.toFixed(4), "good")} ${logtx_pill("Best on this Y scale", logtx_bestModel.has(logtx_modelType) ? "★ yes" : "no", logtx_bestModel.has(logtx_modelType) ? "good" : "")}
` ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false // Scatter plot logtx_scatterPlot = { const { a, b } = logtx_ols; const txArr = logtx_transformed.map(d => d.tx); const xMin = d3.min(txArr), xMax = d3.max(txArr); const lineData = [{ tx: xMin, ty: a + b * xMin }, { tx: xMax, ty: a + b * xMax }]; const pal = logtx_ber640; return Plot.plot(logtx_plotDefaults({ width: 330, height: 300, marginLeft: 48, marginBottom: 40, marginTop: 16, marginRight: 16, style: { color: logtx_theme.text }, x: { label: logtx_xLabel, grid: true }, y: { label: logtx_yLabel, grid: true }, marks: [ Plot.dot(logtx_transformed, { x: "tx", y: "ty", r: 3.5, fill: logtx_withAlpha(pal.primary, 0.5), stroke: pal.primary, strokeWidth: 0.8 }), Plot.line(logtx_loessSmooth, { x: "tx", y: "ty", stroke: logtx_theme.muted, strokeWidth: 2, strokeDasharray: "6,3", strokeOpacity: 0.7 }), Plot.line(lineData, { x: "tx", y: "ty", stroke: pal.secondary, strokeWidth: 2.5 }) ] })); } ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false // R² comparison bar — grouped by Y scale (level-Y on top, log-Y below). // The two groups are NOT cross-comparable, so they read as separate panels // via per-group color + label + a dashed divider. logtx_r2Bar = { const ordered = ["Level–Level", "Level–Log", "Log–Level", "Log–Log"]; const scaleOf = m => (m === "Log–Level" || m === "Log–Log") ? To get coefficients that match the raw polynomial form $\beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2 + \ldots$, use `poly(x, degree, raw = TRUE)`. We use `raw = TRUE` throughout this chapter for interpretability. ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} ggplot(gcse, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.12, color = "grey50") + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, aes(color = "Linear"), se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ poly(x, 2, raw = TRUE), aes(color = "Quadratic"), se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ poly(x, 3, raw = TRUE), aes(color = "Cubic"), se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + scale_color_manual(values = c("Linear" = ber640_colors[["accent"]], "Quadratic" = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], "Cubic" = ber640_colors[["warning"]]), limits = c("Linear", "Quadratic", "Cubic")) + labs( title = "Polynomial Regression Fits", x = "LRT Score", y = "GCSE Score", color = "Model" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Comparing linear, quadratic, and cubic fits to the GCSE-LRT relationship. For these data all three fits are nearly identical and essentially straight, separating only slightly at the LRT extremes.](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/fig-ch6-polynomial-1.png){#fig-ch6-polynomial fig-alt='Scatter plot with LRT score on the x-axis and GCSE score on the y-axis, with three overlaid fitted lines for the linear, quadratic, and cubic models. The cubic fit (gold) adds an inflection point. If the quadratic and cubic curves are nearly identical, the extra cubic term is unnecessary. If the linear line clearly diverges from the polynomial curves, nonlinearity is present and a polynomial model is more appropriate. ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gcse, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.12, color = "grey50") + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, aes(color = "Linear"), se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ poly(x, 2, raw = TRUE), aes(color = "Quadratic"), se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ poly(x, 3, raw = TRUE), aes(color = "Cubic"), se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + scale_color_manual(values = c("Linear" = ber640_colors[["accent"]], "Quadratic" = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], "Cubic" = ber640_colors[["warning"]]), limits = c("Linear", "Quadratic", "Cubic")) + labs( title = "Polynomial Regression Fits", x = "LRT Score", y = "GCSE Score", color = "Model" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-21-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: - `poly(x, 2, raw = TRUE)` fits a quadratic (second-degree) polynomial using raw powers of $x$ - `poly(x, 3, raw = TRUE)` fits a cubic (third-degree) polynomial - Higher-degree polynomials are more flexible but risk **overfitting** ::: ### Comparing Polynomial Models ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} # Nested model comparison m_linear <- lm(gcse ~ lrt, data = gcse) m_quad <- lm(gcse ~ poly(lrt, 2, raw = TRUE), data = gcse) m_cubic <- lm(gcse ~ poly(lrt, 3, raw = TRUE), data = gcse) # F-tests for nested models anova(m_linear, m_quad, m_cubic) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}

::: ::: Each row of the ANOVA output tests whether adding the next polynomial term significantly improves the model. For these data the linear-to-quadratic step is significant ($F = 5.65$, $p = .018$), so the quadratic adds *some* evidence of curvature, while the quadratic-to-cubic step is only borderline ($F = 3.78$, $p = .052$). Borderline results like this are exactly where a single $p$-value should not decide the model: a threshold of $.05$ would keep the cubic, but $.06$ would drop it. Read the $F$-tests alongside the AIC/BIC comparison below, the residual diagnostics, and substantive theory rather than treating one cutoff as decisive. ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Nested model comparison m_linear <- lm(gcse ~ lrt, data = gcse) m_quad <- lm(gcse ~ poly(lrt, 2, raw = TRUE), data = gcse) m_cubic <- lm(gcse ~ poly(lrt, 3, raw = TRUE), data = gcse) # F-tests for nested models anova(m_linear, m_quad, m_cubic) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| Res.Df| RSS| Df| Sum of Sq| F| Pr(>F)| |------:|--------:|--:|---------:|--------:|---------:| | 4057| 263191.3| NA| NA| NA| NA| | 4056| 262825.5| 1| 365.7890| 5.648824| 0.0175132| | 4055| 262581.1| 1| 244.4479| 3.774972| 0.0520939|
::: ::: - `anova()` with multiple models performs sequential $F$-tests comparing each model to the previous one - A significant test for the quadratic term means the relationship has curvature beyond a straight line - A significant cubic term means the relationship has an inflection point - Use the simplest model that adequately fits the data (parsimony principle) ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} # Model comparison using AIC and BIC compare_performance(m_linear, m_quad, m_cubic) |> print_md() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: Comparison of Model Performance Indices |Name | Model | AIC (weights) | AICc (weights) | BIC (weights) | R2 | R2 (adj.) | RMSE | Sigma | |:--------|:-----:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:----:|:---------:|:----:|:-----:| |m_linear | lm | 28458.9 (0.04) | 28458.9 (0.04) | 28477.8 (0.77) | 0.35 | 0.35 | 8.05 | 8.05 | |m_quad | lm | 28455.2 (0.28) | 28455.2 (0.28) | 28480.5 (0.20) | 0.35 | 0.35 | 8.05 | 8.05 | |m_cubic | lm | 28453.4 (0.68) | 28453.5 (0.68) | 28485.0 (0.02) | 0.35 | 0.35 | 8.04 | 8.05 | ::: ::: ### Output Interpretation ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: Comparison of Model Performance Indices |Name | Model | AIC (weights) | AICc (weights) | BIC (weights) | R2 | R2 (adj.) | RMSE | Sigma | |:--------|:-----:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:----:|:---------:|:----:|:-----:| |m_linear | lm | 28458.9 (0.04) | 28458.9 (0.04) | 28477.8 (0.77) | 0.35 | 0.35 | 8.05 | 8.05 | |m_quad | lm | 28455.2 (0.28) | 28455.2 (0.28) | 28480.5 (0.20) | 0.35 | 0.35 | 8.05 | 8.05 | |m_cubic | lm | 28453.4 (0.68) | 28453.5 (0.68) | 28485.0 (0.02) | 0.35 | 0.35 | 8.04 | 8.05 | ::: ::: Look for the model with the lowest AIC and BIC values. AIC tends to favor slightly more complex models, while BIC penalizes complexity more heavily. Here the two criteria **disagree**: AIC favors the cubic model, while BIC favors the linear model --- a common outcome when the competing models fit almost equally well. The $R^2$ values reinforce this: they hover around $0.35$ for all three specifications, so the practical fit differences are small and no model is clearly superior on goodness-of-fit alone. When AIC and BIC point in different directions and $R^2$ is essentially flat, the choice should rest on theory, interpretability, residual diagnostics, and out-of-sample predictive performance rather than on any single statistic. ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Model comparison using AIC and BIC compare_performance(m_linear, m_quad, m_cubic) |> print_md() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: Comparison of Model Performance Indices |Name | Model | AIC (weights) | AICc (weights) | BIC (weights) | R2 | R2 (adj.) | RMSE | Sigma | |:--------|:-----:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:----:|:---------:|:----:|:-----:| |m_linear | lm | 28458.9 (0.04) | 28458.9 (0.04) | 28477.8 (0.77) | 0.35 | 0.35 | 8.05 | 8.05 | |m_quad | lm | 28455.2 (0.28) | 28455.2 (0.28) | 28480.5 (0.20) | 0.35 | 0.35 | 8.05 | 8.05 | |m_cubic | lm | 28453.4 (0.68) | 28453.5 (0.68) | 28485.0 (0.02) | 0.35 | 0.35 | 8.04 | 8.05 | ::: ::: - AIC and BIC penalize model complexity, helping to select the best model - Lower AIC/BIC values indicate a better balance of fit and parsimony ::: ### Interactive Exploration: Polynomial Degree Chooser {.unnumbered} ::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"} ## Try It: How Many Curves Is Too Many? The true data-generating process here is **quadratic** (degree 2). Drag the **Polynomial degree (d)** slider from 1 up toward 8 and watch what happens to the fit statistics; flip the **Show residuals** toggle on to see how the vertical residual segments shrink as the curve bends to chase individual points, and use the **Reset** button to return to the starting degree. We simulate the data here because the lesson requires a *known truth*: only by fixing the true degree (2) ourselves can we label the high-degree wiggles as overfitting rather than real signal --- something no single real dataset can certify. Can you spot where the model starts memorizing noise instead of learning the signal? ::: ```{ojs} //| echo: false import { ber640 as poly_ber640, withAlpha as poly_withAlpha, themeColors as poly_themeColors, plotDefaults as poly_plotDefaults, normalRandom as poly_normalRandom, seq as poly_seq, formatCoef as poly_formatCoef, srSummary as poly_srSummary, resetControls as poly_resetControls, onThemeChange as poly_onThemeChange } from "../ojs/shared.js" ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false poly_theme = poly_themeColors() ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false poly_pill = (label, value, tone = "") => html`${label}${value}` ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false viewof poly_degree = Inputs.range([1, 8], { value: 2, step: 1, label: "Polynomial degree (d)" }) ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false viewof poly_showResiduals = Inputs.toggle({ label: "Show residuals", value: false }) ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false poly_n = 50 poly_xRaw = { function lcg(seed, n) { let s = seed; const vals = []; for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) { s = (s * 1664525 + 1013904223) & 0xffffffff; vals.push((s >>> 0) / 0xffffffff); } return vals; } return lcg(42, poly_n).map(u => u * 10); } poly_epsilon = poly_normalRandom(poly_n, 0, 1.5, 7) // seed 7: adjusted R² and AIC both identify the true degree (2); seed 43 was an unlucky sample where adj R² drifted up to degree 6 poly_trueFunc = (x) => 2 + 0.5 * x - 0.08 * x * x poly_yRaw = poly_xRaw.map((x, i) => poly_trueFunc(x) + poly_epsilon[i]) poly_data = poly_xRaw.map((x, i) => ({ x: x, y: poly_yRaw[i] })) ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false // Matrix operations for polynomial regression poly_matOps = { function transpose(A) { const m = A.length, n = A[0].length; const T = Array.from({length: n}, () => new Array(m)); for (let i = 0; i < m; i++) for (let j = 0; j < n; j++) T[j][i] = A[i][j]; return T; } function multiply(A, B) { const m = A.length, n = B[0].length, p = B.length; const C = Array.from({length: m}, () => new Array(n).fill(0)); for (let i = 0; i < m; i++) for (let j = 0; j < n; j++) for (let k = 0; k < p; k++) C[i][j] += A[i][k] * B[k][j]; return C; } function invert(M) { const n = M.length; const aug = M.map((row, i) => { const r = row.slice(); for (let j = 0; j < n; j++) r.push(i === j ? 1 : 0); return r; }); for (let col = 0; col < n; col++) { let maxRow = col, maxVal = Math.abs(aug[col][col]); for (let row = col + 1; row < n; row++) { if (Math.abs(aug[row][col]) > maxVal) { maxVal = Math.abs(aug[row][col]); maxRow = row; } } [aug[col], aug[maxRow]] = [aug[maxRow], aug[col]]; const pivot = aug[col][col]; if (Math.abs(pivot) < 1e-14) return null; for (let j = 0; j < 2 * n; j++) aug[col][j] /= pivot; for (let row = 0; row < n; row++) { if (row === col) continue; const factor = aug[row][col]; for (let j = 0; j < 2 * n; j++) aug[row][j] -= factor * aug[col][j]; } } return aug.map(row => row.slice(n)); } return { transpose, multiply, invert }; } ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false poly_fit = { const d = poly_degree, n = poly_n, x = poly_xRaw, y = poly_yRaw; // Center the predictor before building the polynomial terms (matches the // "Center Before Polynomial Regression" callout below). Centering subtracts // the mean of x, which sharply reduces the correlation between x, x^2, x^3, // ... and therefore the condition number of XtX, without changing the // fitted values, R^2, Adj R^2, or AIC (same column space). const xMean = x.reduce((s, v) => s + v, 0) / n; const X = x.map(xi => { const xc = xi - xMean; const row = new Array(d + 1); row[0] = 1; for (let k = 1; k <= d; k++) row[k] = Math.pow(xc, k); return row; }); const Xt = poly_matOps.transpose(X); const XtX = poly_matOps.multiply(Xt, X); const XtXinv = poly_matOps.invert(XtX); const yCol = y.map(v => [v]); const Xty = poly_matOps.multiply(Xt, yCol); const beta = poly_matOps.multiply(XtXinv, Xty).map(r => r[0]); const yHat = X.map(row => row.reduce((s, v, k) => s + v * beta[k], 0)); const residuals = y.map((yi, i) => yi - yHat[i]); const RSS = residuals.reduce((s, r) => s + r * r, 0); const yMean = y.reduce((s, v) => s + v, 0) / n; const TSS = y.reduce((s, v) => s + (v - yMean) ** 2, 0); const R2 = 1 - RSS / TSS; const adjR2 = 1 - (1 - R2) * (n - 1) / (n - d - 1); const AIC = n * Math.log(RSS / n) + 2 * (d + 1); const xGrid = poly_seq(0, 10, 200); const curveData = xGrid.map(xi => { // beta is now expressed in centered coordinates, so evaluate the fitted // polynomial at (xi - xMean) but plot it at the original xi position. const xc = xi - xMean; let yp = 0; for (let k = 0; k <= d; k++) yp += beta[k] * Math.pow(xc, k); return { x: xi, y: yp }; }); const trueData = xGrid.map(xi => ({ x: xi, y: poly_trueFunc(xi) })); const residualSegs = x.map((xi, i) => ({ x: xi, y1: y[i], y2: yHat[i] })); return { beta, R2, adjR2, AIC, curveData, trueData, residualSegs }; } poly_bestDegree = { // Data-driven: the degree that MINIMIZES AIC across all selectable degrees, // computed on the centered design (same as poly_fit). AIC penalizes complexity, // so for this quadratic DGP it identifies the true degree (2) rather than the // noise-chasing high-degree fit. The badge below compares the slider to this. const n = poly_n, x = poly_xRaw, y = poly_yRaw; const xMean = x.reduce((s, v) => s + v, 0) / n; let best = 1, bestAIC = Infinity; for (let dd = 1; dd <= 8; dd++) { const X = x.map(xi => { const row = new Array(dd + 1); row[0] = 1; for (let k = 1; k <= dd; k++) row[k] = Math.pow(xi - xMean, k); return row; }); const Xt = poly_matOps.transpose(X); const XtXinv = poly_matOps.invert(poly_matOps.multiply(Xt, X)); if (!XtXinv) continue; const beta = poly_matOps.multiply(XtXinv, poly_matOps.multiply(Xt, y.map(v => [v]))).map(r => r[0]); const yHat = X.map(row => row.reduce((s, v, k) => s + v * beta[k], 0)); const RSS = y.reduce((s, yi, i) => s + (yi - yHat[i]) ** 2, 0); const AIC = n * Math.log(RSS / n) + 2 * (dd + 1); if (AIC < bestAIC) { bestAIC = AIC; best = dd; } } return best; } poly_status = { const d = poly_degree, best = poly_bestDegree; if (d === best) return { label: "Just right", color: poly_ber640.success, tone: "good" }; if (d < best) return { label: "Underfitting", color: poly_ber640.warning, tone: "warning" }; if (d === best + 1) return { label: "Slight overfitting", color: poly_ber640.warning, tone: "warning" }; return { label: "Overfitting", color: poly_ber640.secondary, tone: "bad" }; } ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false poly_reset = poly_resetControls([ { input: viewof poly_degree, value: 2 }, { input: viewof poly_showResiduals, value: false } ], { label: "Reset" }) ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false poly_readouts = html`
