` ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false poly_plot = { const marks = []; if (poly_showResiduals) { marks.push(Plot.link(poly_fit.residualSegs, { x1: "x", y1: "y1", x2: "x", y2: "y2", stroke: poly_withAlpha(poly_ber640.accent, 0.4), strokeWidth: 1 })); } marks.push(Plot.line(poly_fit.trueData, { x: "x", y: "y", stroke: poly_theme.muted, strokeWidth: 2, strokeDasharray: "6,4" })); marks.push(Plot.line(poly_fit.curveData, { x: "x", y: "y", stroke: poly_status.color, strokeWidth: 2.5 })); marks.push(Plot.dot(poly_data, { x: "x", y: "y", r: 3.5, fill: poly_withAlpha(poly_ber640.primary, 0.6), stroke: poly_ber640.primary, strokeWidth: 0.8 })); return Plot.plot(poly_plotDefaults({ width: 680, height: 340, marginTop: 24, marginBottom: 40, marginLeft: 48, marginRight: 16, style: { color: poly_theme.text }, x: { label: "x", domain: [0, 10], grid: true }, y: { label: "y", grid: true }, marks })); } ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false poly_sr = poly_srSummary("ch06-poly", `Polynomial degree ${poly_degree} (best by AIC is ${poly_bestDegree}): ${poly_status.label}. R² ${poly_fit.R2.toFixed(4)}, adjusted R² ${poly_fit.adjR2.toFixed(4)}, AIC ${poly_fit.AIC.toFixed(1)}.` + (poly_showResiduals ? " Residual segments shown." : "")) ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false poly_app = html`
${viewof poly_degree}
${viewof poly_showResiduals}${poly_reset}
${poly_readouts}
${poly_plot}
${poly_sr}
` ``` ::: {.callout-note} ## What you should have seen - Raw R² **never decreases** as you raise the polynomial degree --- a degree-8 fit always looks at least as good in-sample as the true degree-2 fit, so R² alone cannot tell you the right complexity. - Adjusted R² peaks and AIC (which penalizes complexity) is minimized at or near the true degree (2); after that, both criteria deteriorate and correctly flag the higher-degree models as overfit. Turning on the residuals shows the high-degree curve wiggling to chase individual points rather than the underlying trend. - The right degree balances fit against parsimony: past the true degree the model is memorizing noise, which hurts out-of-sample prediction even as the in-sample curve hugs the data more tightly. ::: ## Polynomial Extrapolation Polynomial models can fit the data well within the observed range but behave **wildly** outside it. A cubic curve that fits well between LRT = $-30$ and LRT = $30$ may predict absurd GCSE scores at LRT = $50$. **Never extrapolate** with polynomial models. ::: {.callout-important} ## Center Before Polynomial Regression When fitting polynomial models, **center the predictor** (subtract its mean) before computing polynomial terms. Centering has two benefits: 1. **Reduces multicollinearity**: $x$ and $x^2$ are highly correlated when $x$ is far from zero. Centering moves the mean to zero, substantially reducing the correlation between the linear and quadratic terms. 2. **Makes $\beta_1$ interpretable**: In the uncentered model, $\beta_1$ is the slope when $x = 0$, which may be outside the data range or meaningless. In the centered model, $\beta_1$ is the **slope at the mean of $x$** --- a much more useful quantity. A note on the models above: because `lrt` is already standardized to a mean of about 0, the raw and centered polynomials are nearly identical *here*, so we fit the chapter's polynomial models on raw `lrt` for simplicity. In general, however, center before creating polynomial terms to reduce the correlation between $x$ and $x^2$. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Centering before polynomial regression gcse <- gcse |> mutate(lrt_c = lrt - mean(lrt, na.rm = TRUE)) m_quad_centered <- lm(gcse ~ poly(lrt_c, 2, raw = TRUE), data = gcse) ``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-warning} ## Do Not Interpret Polynomial Coefficients in Isolation In a quadratic model $y = \alpha + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2$, the coefficients $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ should **not** be interpreted separately. The effect of a one-unit increase in $x$ depends on the current value of $x$: $$ \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \beta_1 + 2\beta_2 x $$ {#eq-ch6-marginal} This means the "slope" changes continuously across the range of $x$. To understand the effect at specific values of $x$, use **marginal effects** (e.g., the `marginaleffects` package). See @fig-ch6-marginal-slopes and @fig-ch6-derivative below for visualizations of how the slope varies. ::: ### Residual Diagnostics: Linear vs Quadratic {#sec-ch6-resid} A key diagnostic for detecting nonlinearity is the residual-vs-fitted plot. When a linear model is fit to data with a quadratic relationship, residuals show a characteristic curved ("frown" or "smile") pattern. ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} # Residuals from linear model resid_linear <- data.frame( fitted = fitted(m_linear), residuals = residuals(m_linear) ) # Residuals from quadratic model resid_quad <- data.frame( fitted = fitted(m_quad), residuals = residuals(m_quad) ) p_resid_lin <- ggplot(resid_linear, aes(x = fitted, y = residuals)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.1, color = "grey50") + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey30") + labs(title = "Linear Model Residuals", x = "Fitted Values", y = "Residuals") p_resid_quad <- ggplot(resid_quad, aes(x = fitted, y = residuals)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.1, color = "grey50") + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["success"]], se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey30") + labs(title = "Quadratic Model Residuals", x = "Fitted Values", y = "Residuals") p_resid_lin + p_resid_quad ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Residual-vs-fitted plots comparing the linear model (left) with the quadratic model (right). The linear model's residual LOWESS shows only a very slight downward drift at high fitted values; the curvature is negligible and the quadratic model flattens it further.](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/fig-ch6-resid-comparison-1.png){#fig-ch6-resid-comparison fig-alt='Side-by-side residual-versus-fitted-value plots. The left panel shows residuals from the linear model, whose LOWESS curve stays close to zero with only a very slight downward drift at high fitted values, so any missed curvature is negligible. The right panel shows residuals from the quadratic model, which are more randomly scattered around zero with no systematic curvature, confirming that the quadratic term corrects the nonlinearity.' width=3000} ::: ::: ### Output Interpretation ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-39-1.png){width=3000} ::: ::: In the left panel, look for systematic departures of the LOWESS curve from zero --- a pronounced curved ("frown") pattern is the classic signature of missed nonlinearity, though here the curve is nearly flat with only a slight downward drift at high fitted values. The quadratic model (right panel) should show a flatter LOWESS curve near zero, indicating that the curvature has been captured by the $x^2$ term. If the quadratic residuals still show a pattern, higher-order terms or a different modeling strategy may be needed. ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Residuals from linear model resid_linear <- data.frame( fitted = fitted(m_linear), residuals = residuals(m_linear) ) # Residuals from quadratic model resid_quad <- data.frame( fitted = fitted(m_quad), residuals = residuals(m_quad) ) p_resid_lin <- ggplot(resid_linear, aes(x = fitted, y = residuals)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.1, color = "grey50") + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey30") + labs(title = "Linear Model Residuals", x = "Fitted Values", y = "Residuals") p_resid_quad <- ggplot(resid_quad, aes(x = fitted, y = residuals)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.1, color = "grey50") + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = ber640_colors[["success"]], se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey30") + labs(title = "Quadratic Model Residuals", x = "Fitted Values", y = "Residuals") p_resid_lin + p_resid_quad ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-40-1.png){width=3000} ::: ::: - The left panel plots residuals from the linear model against fitted values, with a LOWESS curve (red) to highlight any systematic pattern - The right panel does the same for the quadratic model - A well-specified model should show residuals randomly scattered around zero with no pattern - The `geom_hline(yintercept = 0)` adds a reference line at zero ::: ### Marginal Effects of the Quadratic Model {#sec-ch6-marginal} Because the slope in a quadratic model changes with $x$, we can visualize **how the effect varies** across the predictor range. ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} plot_slopes(m_quad, variables = "lrt", condition = "lrt") + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") + labs( title = "Marginal Effect of LRT on GCSE (Quadratic Model)", subtitle = "How the slope changes across the LRT range", x = "LRT Score", y = "Marginal Effect (slope of GCSE on LRT)" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Marginal effect (instantaneous slope) of LRT on GCSE across the range of LRT scores, estimated from the quadratic model. The slope rises gently from about 0.47 to 0.73 as LRT increases, indicating a mildly accelerating (slightly increasing-returns) but essentially monotone-increasing relationship over the observed data. The curvature is negligible (the quadratic term adds only about 0.001 to R-squared and is detectable only because n is large).](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/fig-ch6-marginal-slopes-1.png){#fig-ch6-marginal-slopes fig-alt='Line plot with LRT score on the x-axis and the marginal effect of LRT on GCSE on the y-axis. A gently upward-sloping line shows that the instantaneous slope is positive everywhere and rises slightly as LRT increases, from about 0.47 at the lowest LRT values to about 0.73 at the highest. A shaded 95 percent confidence band surrounds the line and stays well above zero throughout. The pattern indicates a slightly accelerating but essentially monotone-increasing relationship: each additional unit of LRT produces a marginally larger gain in GCSE at higher LRT levels, with the overall curvature so small that the line is barely distinguishable from a flat constant slope.' width=2400} ::: ::: ### Output Interpretation ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-41-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: The plot shows how the relationship between LRT and GCSE varies across LRT values. Here the slope line rises gently --- from roughly 0.47 at the lowest LRT scores to roughly 0.73 at the highest --- so the positive effect of LRT on GCSE *grows slightly* at higher LRT scores rather than diminishing. The confidence band stays well above zero across the entire observed range, so the marginal effect is positive and significant everywhere in the data. The point where the slope would cross zero is the **turning point** of the quadratic curve; here it is a *minimum* at LRT $\approx -141$, far below the observed range $[-29.35, 30.16]$, so no turning point appears in the plot. In practical terms the relationship is **monotone increasing** over the data. The curvature is also **negligible**: the quadratic term raises $R^2$ by only about $0.001$ and is "significant" only because the sample is very large ($n = 4059$), so within the data the quadratic is barely distinguishable from a straight line. ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plot_slopes(m_quad, variables = "lrt", condition = "lrt") + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") + labs( title = "Marginal Effect of LRT on GCSE (Quadratic Model)", subtitle = "How the slope changes across the LRT range", x = "LRT Score", y = "Marginal Effect (slope of GCSE on LRT)" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-42-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: - `plot_slopes()` from the `marginaleffects` package computes the instantaneous slope $\partial y / \partial x$ at each value of $x$ - For a quadratic model, this slope is a straight line: $\beta_1 + 2\beta_2 x$ - The horizontal dashed line at zero marks where the effect of LRT would switch sign; here the marginal-effect line stays well above zero across the whole observed range, so the effect never switches sign in the data - The confidence band shows uncertainty in the marginal effect at each point ::: ### Derivative (Instantaneous Slope) Plot {#sec-ch6-derivative} We can also compute and plot the first derivative analytically from the quadratic model coefficients. ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} # Extract coefficients from the quadratic model b <- coef(m_quad) b1 <- b[2] # linear coefficient b2 <- b[3] # quadratic coefficient # Turning point: where derivative = 0 turning_point <- -b1 / (2 * b2) # Create derivative data across the LRT range lrt_range <- range(gcse$lrt, na.rm = TRUE) deriv_df <- data.frame( lrt = seq(lrt_range[1], lrt_range[2], length.out = 200) ) |> mutate(derivative = b1 + 2 * b2 * lrt) ggplot(deriv_df, aes(x = lrt, y = derivative)) + geom_line(color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], linewidth = 1.2) + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") + # The turning point (-b1 / (2 b2)) is a MINIMUM far outside the data range, # so we constrain the plot to the observed LRT range and annotate its location # rather than drawing an off-range vertical line that would strand the curve. annotate("text", x = lrt_range[1], y = max(deriv_df$derivative), label = paste0("Turning point (minimum) at LRT = ", round(turning_point, 1), ", far outside the data"), hjust = 0, vjust = 1, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], fontface = "bold") + coord_cartesian(xlim = lrt_range) + labs( title = "First Derivative of Quadratic Model", subtitle = expression(paste("Instantaneous slope = ", beta[1], " + 2", beta[2], "x")), x = "LRT Score", y = "dy/dx (Instantaneous Slope)" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![First derivative of the quadratic regression function, showing how the instantaneous slope changes linearly across LRT. The line ascends gently and stays positive throughout the observed range, so the slope never reaches zero within the data; the turning point (a minimum) lies at LRT around -141, far outside the data, and is noted in the plot rather than drawn.](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/fig-ch6-derivative-1.png){#fig-ch6-derivative fig-alt='Line plot with LRT score on the x-axis and the first derivative (instantaneous slope) on the y-axis. A straight diagonal line ascends gently from about 0.47 on the left to about 0.73 on the right, staying positive across the whole observed range and reflecting the slight linear increase in slope implied by the convex quadratic model. The line never crosses zero within the data; a text note reports that the turning point --- a minimum --- lies at LRT of about -141, far outside the observed range, so within the data the predicted GCSE score is monotonically increasing.' width=2400} ::: ::: ### Output Interpretation ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-43-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: This plot provides a direct visualization of @eq-ch6-marginal. The $y$-axis shows the instantaneous rate of change of GCSE with respect to LRT at each point. The derivative line is positive across the entire observed range and rises gently (from about 0.47 to about 0.73), so increasing LRT is associated with higher GCSE scores everywhere in the data, and slightly more so at higher LRT. In general, the turning point (where the derivative equals zero) is the LRT score at which the predicted GCSE is maximized (for an inverted-U, $\beta_2 < 0$) or minimized (for a U-shape, $\beta_2 > 0$). Here $\beta_2 > 0$, so the curve is convex and the turning point is a **minimum** at LRT $\approx -141$ --- far outside the observed range $[-29.35, 30.16]$. Because that turning point falls well outside the data, the practical effect within the data is **monotone increasing** (always increasing, at a very slightly accelerating rate). In fact the curvature is **negligible**: the quadratic term raises $R^2$ by only about $0.001$ and is statistically significant only because $n = 4059$ is large, so over the observed range the quadratic is nearly indistinguishable from a straight line. ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Extract coefficients from the quadratic model b <- coef(m_quad) b1 <- b[2] # linear coefficient b2 <- b[3] # quadratic coefficient # Turning point: where derivative = 0 turning_point <- -b1 / (2 * b2) # Create derivative data across the LRT range lrt_range <- range(gcse$lrt, na.rm = TRUE) deriv_df <- data.frame( lrt = seq(lrt_range[1], lrt_range[2], length.out = 200) ) |> mutate(derivative = b1 + 2 * b2 * lrt) ggplot(deriv_df, aes(x = lrt, y = derivative)) + geom_line(color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], linewidth = 1.2) + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") + # The turning point (-b1 / (2 b2)) is a MINIMUM far outside the data range, # so we constrain the plot to the observed LRT range and annotate its location # rather than drawing an off-range vertical line that would strand the curve. annotate("text", x = lrt_range[1], y = max(deriv_df$derivative), label = paste0("Turning point (minimum) at LRT = ", round(turning_point, 1), ", far outside the data"), hjust = 0, vjust = 1, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], fontface = "bold") + coord_cartesian(xlim = lrt_range) + labs( title = "First Derivative of Quadratic Model", subtitle = expression(paste("Instantaneous slope = ", beta[1], " + 2", beta[2], "x")), x = "LRT Score", y = "dy/dx (Instantaneous Slope)" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-44-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: - The first derivative of $y = \alpha + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2$ is $dy/dx = \beta_1 + 2\beta_2 x$, which is a straight line - The **turning point** occurs where $dy/dx = 0$, i.e., at $x = -\beta_1 / (2\beta_2)$ - The sign of $\beta_2$ determines the shape: $\beta_2 > 0$ gives a convex (U-shaped) curve whose turning point is a minimum, while $\beta_2 < 0$ gives a concave (inverted-U) curve whose turning point is a maximum. Here $\beta_2 > 0$ and the minimum lies at LRT $\approx -141$, far below the data, so the slope is positive and *rising* across the entire observed range - Because the turning point ($-\beta_1 / 2\beta_2 \approx -141$) lies far outside the observed LRT range, the plot is constrained to the data via `coord_cartesian()` and the turning point is reported in a text annotation instead of an off-range vertical line ::: ## Piecewise Constant Models {#sec-ch6-piecewise} A piecewise constant model treats a continuous variable as a categorical variable by dividing it into groups. This is essentially one-way ANOVA (@sec-ch3) applied to a discretized predictor. ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} gcse_grouped <- gcse |> mutate(lrt_group = cut(lrt, breaks = quantile(lrt, probs = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), na.rm = TRUE), include.lowest = TRUE, labels = c("Q1 (Low)", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4 (High)") )) |> filter(!is.na(lrt_group)) m_piece <- lm(gcse ~ lrt_group, data = gcse_grouped) plot_predictions(m_piece, condition = "lrt_group") + labs( title = "Piecewise Constant Model", subtitle = "LRT divided into quartile groups", x = "LRT Quartile", y = "Predicted GCSE Score" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Piecewise constant model: predicted mean GCSE (with 95% confidence intervals) for each LRT quartile group, shown as a point-and-interval plot.](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/fig-ch6-piecewise-1.png){#fig-ch6-piecewise fig-alt='Point-and-interval plot with four LRT quartile groups (Q1 Low through Q4 High) on the x-axis and predicted GCSE score on the y-axis. Each group is shown as a point marking its estimated mean GCSE with a vertical 95 percent confidence interval. The predicted means rise from Q1 to Q4, showing a positive trend across the quartile groups.' width=2400} ::: ::: ### Output Interpretation ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-45-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: The plot shows the predicted mean GCSE score for each LRT quartile group, with confidence intervals. Each bar or point represents the estimated group mean. If the means increase steadily from Q1 to Q4, the relationship is monotonically positive. If the jump between adjacent groups is uneven (e.g., a large jump from Q1 to Q2 but a small jump from Q3 to Q4), this suggests the relationship is not linear --- the effect of LRT on GCSE is stronger at lower LRT values. Compare these group means to the smooth polynomial curve: the piecewise model captures the general trend but misses within-group variation. The confidence intervals help you judge whether adjacent groups are statistically distinguishable. ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} gcse_grouped <- gcse |> mutate(lrt_group = cut(lrt, breaks = quantile(lrt, probs = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), na.rm = TRUE), include.lowest = TRUE, labels = c("Q1 (Low)", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4 (High)") )) |> filter(!is.na(lrt_group)) m_piece <- lm(gcse ~ lrt_group, data = gcse_grouped) plot_predictions(m_piece, condition = "lrt_group") + labs( title = "Piecewise Constant Model", subtitle = "LRT divided into quartile groups", x = "LRT Quartile", y = "Predicted GCSE Score" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-46-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: - `cut()` divides LRT into quartile groups based on the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles - The model estimates a separate mean for each group --- this is just one-way ANOVA (@sec-ch3) - Piecewise models are useful when the relationship is step-like or when the true functional form is unknown - The tradeoff: piecewise models lose information about within-group variation ::: ## Choosing Among Approaches {#sec-ch6-choosing} | Approach | Best for | Advantages | Disadvantages | |:---------|:---------|:-----------|:-------------| | **LOWESS** | Exploration and detection | No assumptions | Not a formal model; cannot predict | | **Log** | Multiplicative relationships, wide ranges | Simple interpretation | Requires positive values | | **Polynomial** | Smooth curves | Flexible within data range | Extrapolation danger; overfitting | | **Piecewise** | Step-like relationships | Simple; no functional form assumption | Loses within-group information | ### Model Comparison Overlay {#sec-ch6-model-overlay} ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} # Generate prediction grids lrt_seq <- seq(min(gcse$lrt, na.rm = TRUE), max(gcse$lrt, na.rm = TRUE), length.out = 300) pred_linear <- data.frame( lrt = lrt_seq, gcse = predict(m_linear, newdata = data.frame(lrt = lrt_seq)), model = "Linear" ) pred_quad <- data.frame( lrt = lrt_seq, gcse = predict(m_quad, newdata = data.frame(lrt = lrt_seq)), model = "Quadratic" ) # Piecewise predictions: assign each lrt_seq value to a quartile group pred_piece_df <- data.frame(lrt = lrt_seq) |> mutate(lrt_group = cut(lrt, breaks = quantile(gcse$lrt, probs = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), na.rm = TRUE), include.lowest = TRUE, labels = c("Q1 (Low)", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4 (High)") )) |> filter(!is.na(lrt_group)) pred_piece <- data.frame( lrt = pred_piece_df$lrt, gcse = predict(m_piece, newdata = pred_piece_df), model = "Piecewise" ) # Combine predictions pred_all <- bind_rows(pred_linear, pred_quad, pred_piece) ggplot() + geom_point(data = gcse, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse), alpha = 0.05, color = "grey50") + geom_smooth(data = gcse, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse), method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = "grey40", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dotted") + geom_line(data = pred_all, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse, color = model, linetype = model), linewidth = 1.1) + scale_color_manual( values = c("Linear" = ber640_colors[["accent"]], "Quadratic" = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], "Piecewise" = ber640_colors[["success"]]) ) + scale_linetype_manual( values = c("Linear" = "solid", "Quadratic" = "dashed", "Piecewise" = "dotdash") ) + labs( title = "Model Comparison: Linear vs Quadratic vs Piecewise", subtitle = "Grey dotted line = LOWESS nonparametric benchmark", x = "LRT Score", y = "Predicted GCSE Score", color = "Model", linetype = "Model" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Predictions from linear, quadratic, and piecewise models overlaid on the GCSE-LRT scatterplot, with a LOWESS curve as a nonparametric benchmark. Comparing the fitted curves helps identify which parametric model best captures the underlying relationship.](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/fig-ch6-model-comparison-1.png){#fig-ch6-model-comparison fig-alt='Scatter plot with LRT score on the x-axis and GCSE score on the y-axis showing four overlaid prediction curves: a straight linear fit, a smooth quadratic curve, a step-function piecewise fit, and a LOWESS curve serving as a nonparametric benchmark. The quadratic curve and LOWESS track each other closely, while the linear fit misses curvature at the extremes and the piecewise model approximates the trend with discrete jumps.' width=3000} ::: ::: ### Output Interpretation ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-47-1.png){width=3000} ::: ::: Compare how closely each parametric model follows the LOWESS benchmark (grey dotted curve). The linear model will diverge wherever the true relationship curves. The quadratic model should track the LOWESS more closely if there is genuine curvature, particularly at the extremes of the LRT range. The piecewise model approximates the curve in broad steps --- it captures the correct direction but introduces artificial jumps at the quartile boundaries. The model whose predictions most closely match the LOWESS curve across the entire range is likely the best choice, though parsimony and interpretability also matter. Note that the piecewise model may appear better in some regions and worse in others compared to the polynomial. ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Generate prediction grids lrt_seq <- seq(min(gcse$lrt, na.rm = TRUE), max(gcse$lrt, na.rm = TRUE), length.out = 300) pred_linear <- data.frame( lrt = lrt_seq, gcse = predict(m_linear, newdata = data.frame(lrt = lrt_seq)), model = "Linear" ) pred_quad <- data.frame( lrt = lrt_seq, gcse = predict(m_quad, newdata = data.frame(lrt = lrt_seq)), model = "Quadratic" ) # Piecewise predictions: assign each lrt_seq value to a quartile group pred_piece_df <- data.frame(lrt = lrt_seq) |> mutate(lrt_group = cut(lrt, breaks = quantile(gcse$lrt, probs = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), na.rm = TRUE), include.lowest = TRUE, labels = c("Q1 (Low)", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4 (High)") )) |> filter(!is.na(lrt_group)) pred_piece <- data.frame( lrt = pred_piece_df$lrt, gcse = predict(m_piece, newdata = pred_piece_df), model = "Piecewise" ) # Combine predictions pred_all <- bind_rows(pred_linear, pred_quad, pred_piece) ggplot() + geom_point(data = gcse, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse), alpha = 0.05, color = "grey50") + geom_smooth(data = gcse, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse), method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = "grey40", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dotted") + geom_line(data = pred_all, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse, color = model, linetype = model), linewidth = 1.1) + scale_color_manual( values = c("Linear" = ber640_colors[["accent"]], "Quadratic" = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], "Piecewise" = ber640_colors[["success"]]) ) + scale_linetype_manual( values = c("Linear" = "solid", "Quadratic" = "dashed", "Piecewise" = "dotdash") ) + labs( title = "Model Comparison: Linear vs Quadratic vs Piecewise", subtitle = "Grey dotted line = LOWESS nonparametric benchmark", x = "LRT Score", y = "Predicted GCSE Score", color = "Model", linetype = "Model" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-48-1.png){width=3000} ::: ::: - **Prediction grids**: We generate predictions from each model across the full LRT range using `predict()`, then combine them into a single data frame for plotting - **LOWESS benchmark**: The grey dotted LOWESS curve provides a nonparametric reference --- the closer a parametric model follows it, the better it captures the true relationship - **Linear model** (solid blue): Assumes a constant slope; misses any curvature - **Quadratic model** (dashed red): Allows a single bend, capturing diminishing returns or a turning point - **Piecewise model** (dot-dash teal): Produces step-like predictions within quartile groups; captures the general trend but not smooth transitions - Different linetypes make models distinguishable even in black-and-white printing ::: ::: {.callout-note} ## When to Use What: Education Research Examples Choosing the right nonlinear specification depends on the theoretical relationship you expect: a. **Diminishing returns $\rightarrow$ Log transformation**: Extra tutoring hours boost achievement, but each additional hour matters less. A level-log model $y = \alpha + \beta \log(\text{hours})$ naturally captures this pattern --- the first 5 hours matter much more than hours 25--30. b. **U-shaped or inverted-U $\rightarrow$ Quadratic polynomial**: Class size and achievement may follow an inverted-U: very small and very large classes both underperform mid-sized ones. A quadratic model $y = \alpha + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2$ captures this turning point. c. **Threshold effects $\rightarrow$ Piecewise model**: Grade-level reading benchmarks create natural "steps" --- students who cross a proficiency threshold may show a discrete jump in outcomes. Piecewise models align with the policy-relevant cutpoints. d. **No strong theory $\rightarrow$ LOWESS first**: When you have no prior expectation about the functional form, start with LOWESS to let the data speak. Then choose the parametric model that best matches the LOWESS shape. ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Practical Strategy 1. **Start** with a scatter plot and LOWESS curve to **detect** nonlinearity 2. Let **theory** guide the choice of model: if diminishing returns, try log; if U-shaped, try quadratic 3. **Compare** models using $F$-tests (`anova()`) or information criteria (AIC, BIC) 4. Always check that the chosen model fits within the observed data range 5. Consider **centering** predictors before adding polynomial terms to reduce collinearity ::: ## Spline Regression {#sec-ch6-spline} Before introducing splines, note a shift in the running example. The polynomial section used the full GCSE dataset; from here on the spline and GAM examples focus on a single school (Battersea, school 15) to isolate the LRT--GCSE shape within one context. With this smaller sample, the flexible fits below are used mainly for illustration rather than as definitive models. The polynomial models from the previous section are a natural first tool for capturing curvature, but they have two important limitations that become apparent as the degree increases: 1. **Runge phenomenon**: High-degree polynomials tend to oscillate wildly near the boundaries of the data. Adding a 6th- or 7th-degree term to capture a local wiggle can create large, spurious swings at the edges of the $x$ range --- exactly where we can least afford instability. 2. **Global influence**: Every polynomial coefficient affects the entire fitted curve. If the relationship between $x$ and $y$ bends sharply in one region, a polynomial must adjust *everywhere* to accommodate that local feature, often producing a poor fit in regions where the data are well-behaved. **Splines** address both problems by stitching together low-degree polynomials across subregions of the predictor, joined at points called **knots**. Formally, a regression spline of degree $d$ with knots $\kappa_1, \kappa_2, \ldots, \kappa_K$ is a piecewise polynomial of degree $d$ that is constrained to be continuous (and, for cubic splines, to have continuous first and second derivatives) at each knot. The result is a smooth curve that adapts locally without the boundary instability of a single high-degree polynomial. The most widely used variant in applied work is the **natural cubic spline**, which adds an additional constraint: the fitted curve is forced to be *linear* beyond the outermost knots. This prevents the extrapolation disasters that plague ordinary polynomials. ### Mathematical Formulation A natural cubic spline with $K$ interior knots uses $K + 1$ degrees of freedom (one for each basis function). In R, we specify this through the `df` argument in `splines::ns()`: $$ y_i = \alpha + \sum_{j=1}^{\text{df}} \beta_j \, B_j(x_i) + \epsilon_i $$ {#eq-ch6-spline} where $B_j(x)$ are the natural spline basis functions. The `df` parameter equals the number of basis functions: `df = 4` places 3 interior knots (at data-driven locations, typically quantiles), yielding a smooth curve with 4 basis functions. ### Fitting a Natural Spline ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} library(splines) # Subset to Battersea school for spline demonstrations battersea <- gcse |> filter(school == 15) # Fit models m_spline <- lm(gcse ~ ns(lrt, df = 4), data = battersea) m_poly5 <- lm(gcse ~ poly(lrt, 5, raw = TRUE), data = battersea) # Prediction grid lrt_grid <- data.frame( lrt = seq(min(battersea$lrt), max(battersea$lrt), length.out = 200) ) lrt_grid$spline_pred <- predict(m_spline, newdata = lrt_grid) lrt_grid$poly5_pred <- predict(m_poly5, newdata = lrt_grid) ggplot(battersea, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "grey50") + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = "grey40", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dotted") + geom_line(data = lrt_grid, aes(y = spline_pred), color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], linewidth = 1.1) + geom_line(data = lrt_grid, aes(y = poly5_pred), color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") + labs( title = "Natural Spline vs Degree-5 Polynomial", subtitle = "Blue = spline (df=4) | Red dashed = polynomial (degree 5) | Grey dotted = LOWESS", x = "LRT Score", y = "GCSE Score" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Natural cubic spline (df = 4) compared with a degree-5 polynomial and a LOWESS curve on the Battersea school data. The spline tracks the LOWESS benchmark closely without the boundary oscillation of the polynomial.](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/fig-ch6-spline-vs-poly-1.png){#fig-ch6-spline-vs-poly fig-alt='Scatter plot of GCSE score versus LRT score for Battersea school with three overlaid fitted curves: a blue natural cubic spline with four degrees of freedom, a red dashed degree-five polynomial, and a grey dotted LOWESS curve. The spline and LOWESS curves align closely throughout the data range. The polynomial diverges noticeably near the edges.' width=2400} ::: ::: ### Output Interpretation ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-49-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: - The **natural spline** (blue solid) tracks the LOWESS benchmark closely across the entire LRT range, including at the boundaries. - The **degree-5 polynomial** (red dashed) may diverge at the extremes, illustrating the Runge phenomenon. - When the spline and LOWESS nearly coincide, the two flexible fits agree on the shape under the chosen tuning, which increases confidence that the pattern is not a one-off artifact of a single parametric choice. Agreement does **not**, however, prove that this is the true functional form or that the spline is free of overfitting --- both methods could be smoothing the same idiosyncrasies in the same way. ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(splines) # Subset to Battersea school for spline demonstrations battersea <- gcse |> filter(school == 15) # Fit models m_spline <- lm(gcse ~ ns(lrt, df = 4), data = battersea) m_poly5 <- lm(gcse ~ poly(lrt, 5, raw = TRUE), data = battersea) # Prediction grid lrt_grid <- data.frame( lrt = seq(min(battersea$lrt), max(battersea$lrt), length.out = 200) ) lrt_grid$spline_pred <- predict(m_spline, newdata = lrt_grid) lrt_grid$poly5_pred <- predict(m_poly5, newdata = lrt_grid) ggplot(battersea, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "grey50") + geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y ~ x, color = "grey40", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dotted") + geom_line(data = lrt_grid, aes(y = spline_pred), color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], linewidth = 1.1) + geom_line(data = lrt_grid, aes(y = poly5_pred), color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") + labs( title = "Natural Spline vs Degree-5 Polynomial", subtitle = "Blue = spline (df=4) | Red dashed = polynomial (degree 5) | Grey dotted = LOWESS", x = "LRT Score", y = "GCSE Score" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-50-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: - `splines::ns(lrt, df = 4)` creates a natural cubic spline basis matrix with 4 degrees of freedom (3 interior knots placed at data quantiles) - The spline is fit inside an ordinary `lm()` call --- no special estimation is needed - `poly(lrt, 5, raw = TRUE)` fits a degree-5 polynomial for comparison - The LOWESS curve serves as a nonparametric benchmark ::: ### Choosing Degrees of Freedom ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {#tbl-ch6-spline-df .cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} # Compare natural spline models with df = 2 through 6 spline_fits <- tibble(df = 2:6) |> mutate( model = map(df, \(d) lm(gcse ~ ns(lrt, df = d), data = battersea)), AIC = map_dbl(model, AIC), BIC = map_dbl(model, BIC), R2 = map_dbl(model, \(m) summary(m)$r.squared) ) spline_fits |> select(df, AIC, BIC, R2) |> mutate(across(c(AIC, BIC), \(x) round(x, 1)), R2 = round(R2, 4)) |> knitr::kable( col.names = c("df", "AIC", "BIC", "R²"), caption = "AIC, BIC, and R² for natural splines with varying degrees of freedom" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: AIC, BIC, and R² for natural splines with varying degrees of freedom | df| AIC| BIC| R²| |--:|-----:|-----:|------:| | 2| 633.4| 643.4| 0.3809| | 3| 634.7| 647.3| 0.3855| | 4| 636.1| 651.2| 0.3896| | 5| 637.9| 655.5| 0.3907| | 6| 639.5| 659.6| 0.3937| ::: ::: ### Output Interpretation ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: AIC, BIC, and R² for natural splines with varying degrees of freedom | df| AIC| BIC| R²| |--:|-----:|-----:|------:| | 2| 633.4| 643.4| 0.3809| | 3| 634.7| 647.3| 0.3855| | 4| 636.1| 651.2| 0.3896| | 5| 637.9| 655.5| 0.3907| | 6| 639.5| 659.6| 0.3937| ::: ::: - AIC tends to select a more flexible model (higher `df`); BIC penalizes complexity more and prefers parsimony - In most education research applications, `df = 3` or `df = 4` provides a good balance ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Compare natural spline models with df = 2 through 6 spline_fits <- tibble(df = 2:6) |> mutate( model = map(df, \(d) lm(gcse ~ ns(lrt, df = d), data = battersea)), AIC = map_dbl(model, AIC), BIC = map_dbl(model, BIC), R2 = map_dbl(model, \(m) summary(m)$r.squared) ) spline_fits |> select(df, AIC, BIC, R2) |> mutate(across(c(AIC, BIC), \(x) round(x, 1)), R2 = round(R2, 4)) |> knitr::kable( col.names = c("df", "AIC", "BIC", "R²"), caption = "AIC, BIC, and R² for natural splines with varying degrees of freedom" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: AIC, BIC, and R² for natural splines with varying degrees of freedom | df| AIC| BIC| R²| |--:|-----:|-----:|------:| | 2| 633.4| 643.4| 0.3809| | 3| 634.7| 647.3| 0.3855| | 4| 636.1| 651.2| 0.3896| | 5| 637.9| 655.5| 0.3907| | 6| 639.5| 659.6| 0.3937| ::: ::: - We fit five spline models with `df` from 2 to 6, each inside a standard `lm()` call - AIC and BIC are extracted for model comparison; lower values indicate better fit-complexity tradeoff - $R^2$ shows where gains plateau ::: ::: {.callout-important} ## Spline Coefficients Are Not Directly Interpretable Unlike a polynomial model where $\beta_2$ captures curvature, spline basis coefficients correspond to abstract basis functions. To understand the fitted relationship: 1. **Plot predictions** across the range of $x$ 2. **Compute marginal effects** at specific $x$ values using `marginaleffects::slopes()` 3. **Report the overall F-test** for the spline term ::: ## Piecewise Linear Regression {#sec-ch6-piecewise-linear} The piecewise constant model in @sec-ch6-piecewise approximated the relationship with flat steps. **Piecewise linear regression** (also called "broken stick" regression) retains a slope within each segment while allowing the slope to change at specified **knot** points. ### The Model A piecewise linear model with a single knot at $\kappa$ is: $$ y_i = \alpha + \beta_1 x_i + \beta_2 (x_i - \kappa)_+ + \epsilon_i $$ {#eq-ch6-piecewise-linear} where $(x_i - \kappa)_+ = \max(x_i - \kappa, 0)$. This ensures: - For $x < \kappa$: slope is $\beta_1$ - For $x \geq \kappa$: slope is $\beta_1 + \beta_2$ - The function is **continuous** at the knot (no jump, only a slope change) ### Fitting a Piecewise Linear Model ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} # Define knot at the median LRT knot <- median(battersea$lrt) # Piecewise linear model using pmax() m_pwl <- lm(gcse ~ lrt + I(pmax(lrt - knot, 0)), data = battersea) # Predictions lrt_grid$pwl_pred <- predict(m_pwl, newdata = lrt_grid) ggplot(battersea, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "grey50") + geom_vline(xintercept = knot, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60") + geom_line(data = lrt_grid, aes(y = pwl_pred), color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], linewidth = 1.2) + annotate("text", x = knot + 0.3, y = max(battersea$gcse, na.rm = TRUE), label = paste0("Knot = ", round(knot, 1)), hjust = 0, color = "grey40", size = 3.5) + labs( title = "Piecewise Linear Regression", subtitle = "Slope changes at the median LRT", x = "LRT Score", y = "GCSE Score" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Piecewise linear fit with a knot at the median LRT score. For the Battersea data the slope change at the knot is small, so the fit looks nearly straight; the fitted function remains continuous at the knot.](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/fig-ch6-pwl-fit-1.png){#fig-ch6-pwl-fit fig-alt='Scatter plot of GCSE score versus LRT score with a piecewise linear fit shown as two connected line segments meeting at the median LRT value, marked by a vertical dashed grey line. The slope change at the knot is slight, so the two segments look nearly collinear (almost a single straight line).' width=2400} ::: ::: ### Output Interpretation ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-53-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: The plot shows two joined line segments. The coefficient on `I(pmax(lrt - knot, 0))` gives the *change* in slope at the knot: positive means the relationship steepens, negative means it flattens. A significant coefficient indicates the slope genuinely changes at that point. ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Define knot at the median LRT knot <- median(battersea$lrt) # Piecewise linear model using pmax() m_pwl <- lm(gcse ~ lrt + I(pmax(lrt - knot, 0)), data = battersea) # Predictions lrt_grid$pwl_pred <- predict(m_pwl, newdata = lrt_grid) ggplot(battersea, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "grey50") + geom_vline(xintercept = knot, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60") + geom_line(data = lrt_grid, aes(y = pwl_pred), color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], linewidth = 1.2) + annotate("text", x = knot + 0.3, y = max(battersea$gcse, na.rm = TRUE), label = paste0("Knot = ", round(knot, 1)), hjust = 0, color = "grey40", size = **Piecewise constant**: Quartile-based step function from @sec-ch6-piecewise - **Piecewise linear**: Broken line with a knot at the median - **Natural spline**: Smooth curve with 4 degrees of freedom ::: ## Generalized Additive Models {#sec-ch6-gam} The methods above all require choosing a specific functional form. **Generalized Additive Models (GAMs)** take a different approach: they estimate the shape of the nonlinear relationship directly from the data, with the smoothness selected automatically via penalized likelihood. $$ y_i = \alpha + f(x_i) + \epsilon_i $$ {#eq-ch6-gam} where $f(\cdot)$ is estimated from the data using a rich spline basis with a **roughness penalty**. The penalty strength is chosen by generalized cross-validation (GCV) or restricted maximum likelihood (REML). ### Effective Degrees of Freedom The model's complexity is summarized by the **effective degrees of freedom (edf)**: edf $\approx 1$ means a straight line, edf $\approx 2$ is roughly quadratic, and higher values indicate more curvature. The edf is estimated, not preset. ### Fitting a GAM ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} library(mgcv) m_gam <- gam(gcse ~ s(lrt), data = battersea) # Summary summary(m_gam) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Family: gaussian Link function: identity Formula: gcse ~ s(lrt) Parametric coefficients: Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|) (Intercept) -0.4061 0.7968 -0.51 0.612 Approximate significance of smooth terms: edf Ref.df F p-value s(lrt) 1 1 54.76 <2e-16 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 R-sq.(adj) = 0.374 Deviance explained = 38.1% GCV = 59.08 Scale est. = 57.782 n = 91 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"} # Predictions with SE gam_preds <- predict(m_gam, newdata = lrt_grid, se.fit = TRUE) lrt_grid$gam_pred <- gam_preds$fit lrt_grid$gam_se <- gam_preds$se.fit edf_val <- round(summary(m_gam)$s.table[, "edf"], 2) ggplot(battersea, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "grey50") + geom_ribbon(data = lrt_grid, aes(y = gam_pred, ymin = gam_pred - 1.96 * gam_se, ymax = gam_pred + 1.96 * gam_se), fill = ber640_colors[["accent"]], alpha = 0.2) + geom_line(data = lrt_grid, aes(y = gam_pred), color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], linewidth = 1.2) + annotate("text", x = min(battersea$lrt) + 1, y = max(battersea$gcse, na.rm = TRUE), label = paste0("edf = ", edf_val), hjust = 0, size = 4, fontface = "italic", color = "grey30") + labs(title = "GAM: Smooth Function of LRT", subtitle = "Shaded region = 95% confidence band", x = "LRT Score", y = "GCSE Score") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![GAM smooth function for the GCSE-LRT relationship, with 95% confidence band. Error t value Pr(>|t|) (Intercept) -0.4061 0.7968 -0.51 0.612 Approximate significance of smooth terms: edf Ref.df F p-value s(lrt) 1 1 54.76 <2e-16 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 R-sq.(adj) = 0.374 Deviance explained = 38.1% GCV = 59.08 Scale est. = 57.782 n = 91 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-57-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: - **edf** close to 1: the data support a nearly linear relationship - **edf** $\approx 2$: roughly quadratic complexity - **edf** $> 3$: complex nonlinearity detected - The confidence band widens where data are sparse ### Code Explanation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(mgcv) m_gam <- gam(gcse ~ s(lrt), data = battersea) # Summary summary(m_gam) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Family: gaussian Link function: identity Formula: gcse ~ s(lrt) Parametric coefficients: Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|) (Intercept) -0.4061 0.7968 -0.51 0.612 Approximate significance of smooth terms: edf Ref.df F p-value s(lrt) 1 1 54.76 <2e-16 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 R-sq.(adj) = 0.374 Deviance explained = 38.1% GCV = 59.08 Scale est. = 57.782 n = 91 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Predictions with SE gam_preds <- predict(m_gam, newdata = lrt_grid, se.fit = TRUE) lrt_grid$gam_pred <- gam_preds$fit lrt_grid$gam_se <- gam_preds$se.fit edf_val <- round(summary(m_gam)$s.table[, "edf"], 2) ggplot(battersea, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "grey50") + geom_ribbon(data = lrt_grid, aes(y = gam_pred, ymin = gam_pred - 1.96 * gam_se, ymax = gam_pred + 1.96 * gam_se), fill = ber640_colors[["accent"]], alpha = 0.2) + geom_line(data = lrt_grid, aes(y = gam_pred), color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], linewidth = 1.2) + annotate("text", x = min(battersea$lrt) + 1, y = max(battersea$gcse, na.rm = TRUE), label = paste0("edf = ", edf_val), hjust = 0, size = 4, fontface = "italic", color = "grey30") + labs(title = "GAM: Smooth Function of LRT", subtitle = "Shaded region = 95% confidence band", x = "LRT Score", y = "GCSE Score") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](06-nonlinear-relationships_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-58-1.png){width=2400} ::: ::: - `mgcv::gam()` fits the model; `s(lrt)` specifies a smooth function using thin-plate regression splines - Smoothing parameter selected automatically by GCV (generalized cross-validation), the default for `mgcv::gam()`; the rendered output reports the resulting GCV score (REML is often recommended in applied GAM work, and can be requested with `method = "REML"`) - `predict(..., se.fit = TRUE)` provides fitted values and standard errors for the confidence band - The edf indicates estimated complexity ::: ::: {.callout-note} ## When to Use GAMs vs Parametric Models - **GAM (exploratory)**: When you don't know the functional form and want the data to reveal it. The edf tells you how complex the relationship is. - **Parametric model (confirmatory)**: When theory specifies a form (diminishing returns → log; U-shape → quadratic). **Practical workflow**: Fit a GAM first, inspect the edf and shape, then choose a parametric model. If edf $\approx 1$, linear suffices. If edf $\approx 2$, quadratic works. If edf $> 3$, consider a natural spline. ::: ## Summary and Key Takeaways {#sec-ch6-summary} ::: {.callout-important} ## Key Takeaways 1. **LOWESS** is a diagnostic tool for detecting nonlinearity --- compare it to a linear fit 2. **Log transformations** are appropriate for multiplicative relationships; interpretation shifts to percentages 3. **Polynomial regression** adds curvature while remaining within the linear regression framework; compare nested models with $F$-tests 4. **Piecewise models** handle step-like relationships by discretizing continuous predictors (equivalent to ANOVA) 5. All these approaches remain "linear" models --- they are linear in the *parameters*, even when nonlinear in the predictors 6. **Never extrapolate** beyond the observed data range, especially with polynomial models 7. Let **theory** guide the choice: if there is a reason to expect diminishing returns, use logs; if a U-shape, use a quadratic ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Looking Ahead The tools in this chapter let you model nonlinear relationships, but several natural questions remain: - **Chapter 7 (Model Building)** formalizes the model comparison process introduced here. You will learn systematic approaches for choosing among competing models using nested $F$-tests, AIC/BIC, and adjusted $R^2$ --- the same tools we used informally to compare polynomial degrees. - **Chapter 8 (Model Diagnostics)** provides systematic tools for detecting nonlinearity (among other violations). The residual-vs-fitted plots used in @sec-ch6-resid are formalized into a complete diagnostic workflow, including component-plus-residual plots that can suggest the specific form of nonlinearity. ::: ## Exercises {#sec-ch6-exercises} ::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"} ## Exercise 6.1: Log Transformation Using the `crime` data, fit both a level-level and log-log model of crime rate on urbanization. Which model provides a better fit? Because the two models have different response scales ($y$ versus $\log y$), their $R^2$ values are **not** directly comparable; judge fit instead by comparing the residual plots (which LOWESS curve sits closer to zero) and the back-transformed prediction error on the original crime-rate scale (e.g., RMSE after exponentiating the fitted log-values). ::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Hint for Exercise 6.1 Start by fitting the two models: `lm(crime_rate ~ urbanization, data = crime_df)` and `lm(log(crime_rate) ~ log(urbanization), data = crime_df)`. You can inspect each model's $R^2$ using `glance()` from `broom`, but do not rank the two models by those cross-scale $R^2$ values. For residual plots, create residual-vs-fitted scatterplots for each model and overlay a LOWESS curve. The model with a flatter LOWESS curve in the residual plot (closer to zero) has better-specified functional form; for predictive comparison, use back-transformed prediction error on the original crime-rate scale. ::: ::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"} ## Exercise 6.2: Polynomial Degree Selection For the GCSE-LRT relationship, test whether a quadratic or cubic term significantly improves the linear model. What is the most parsimonious adequate model? ::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Hint for Exercise 6.2 Use `anova(m_linear, m_quad, m_cubic)` for sequential $F$-tests and `compare_performance(m_linear, m_quad, m_cubic)` for AIC/BIC comparison. A significant $p$-value in the ANOVA table means the additional term improves fit. The most parsimonious model is the simplest one where further terms are not significant. Check whether AIC/BIC agree with the $F$-test conclusion. ::: ::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"} ## Exercise 6.3: Piecewise vs Polynomial Compare the piecewise constant model (LRT in quartiles) with the quadratic polynomial model. Which explains more variance? What are the tradeoffs? ::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Hint for Exercise 6.3 Compare `summary(m_piece)$r.squared` and `summary(m_quad)$r.squared` to see which explains more variance. For tradeoffs, consider: (1) the piecewise model uses 3 degrees of freedom (4 groups minus 1) while the quadratic uses 2 --- which is more parsimonious? (2) The piecewise model makes no assumption about functional form within groups, while the quadratic assumes a smooth curve. (3) The piecewise model loses information about where each student falls within a quartile. Visualize both predictions overlaid on the scatterplot (see @fig-ch6-model-comparison for inspiration). ::: ::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"} ## Exercise 6.4: Piecewise Regression with Different Knot Locations Using the GCSE data, create piecewise constant models with two different grouping strategies: (a) quartiles (4 groups, as in @sec-ch6-piecewise) and (b) terciles (3 groups, splitting at the 33rd and 67th percentiles). Compare the two piecewise models using $R^2$ and AIC. Then compare both to the quadratic polynomial model. What does this exercise tell you about the sensitivity of piecewise models to the choice of knot locations? ::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Hint for Exercise 6.4 For the tercile model, use `cut(lrt, breaks = quantile(lrt, probs = c(0, 1/3, 2/3, 1), na.rm = TRUE), include.lowest = TRUE, labels = c("Low", "Mid", "High"))`. Fit the model with `lm()` and extract $R^2$ with `glance()`. To compare AIC across all three models, use `AIC(m_piece_quartile, m_piece_tercile, m_quad)`. Consider that the quartile model has more parameters (3 dummy variables) than the tercile model (2 dummy variables) --- does the extra parameter justify the improvement in fit? The key insight is that piecewise models are sensitive to where you place the cut points, while polynomial models avoid this arbitrary choice. ::: ::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"} ## Exercise 6.5: When Does a Log Transformation Change Substantive Conclusions? (Conceptual) A researcher models the relationship between school funding (in dollars) and student test scores. In the level-level model, every additional $\$1{,}000$ in per-pupil funding is associated with a 2-point increase in test scores. In the log-level model $\log(Y) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$, the coefficient is $\beta_1 = 0.003$. Are these two models telling the same story or a fundamentally different one? What assumption does the level-level model make about the returns to funding that the log-level model relaxes? Which model would you prefer if you suspected that the benefit of additional funding diminishes for already well-funded schools? ::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Hint for Exercise 6.5 The level-level model assumes constant absolute returns: each dollar buys the same test score improvement regardless of current funding level. The log-level model implies constant percentage returns: each dollar increases scores by a fixed percentage, meaning the absolute gain shrinks as the baseline score grows. If you believe in diminishing returns --- that going from $\$5{,}000$ to $\$6{,}000$ matters more than going from $\$15{,}000$ to $\$16{,}000$ --- the log transformation on $Y$ captures this naturally. Consider which assumption is more realistic for education policy. ::: ::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"} ## Exercise 6.6: Extrapolation Dangers with Polynomial Models (Conceptual) A researcher fits a cubic polynomial model relating student age (range: 6--18) to reading comprehension scores. The model fits the observed data well, with $R^2 = 0.82$. A policy analyst uses this model to predict reading scores for 4-year-olds (to evaluate a pre-K program) and for 22-year-olds (to evaluate college literacy). Why are both extrapolations dangerous, and why is the cubic polynomial especially risky compared to a linear model for extrapolation? What would you recommend instead? ::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Hint for Exercise 6.6 Polynomial models are flexible within the observed range but can behave wildly outside it. A cubic function has two turning points and will eventually shoot toward positive or negative infinity in both directions. Even a small curvature in the data can lead to dramatic swings when extrapolated. Linear models are also unreliable for extrapolation, but at least they continue in a predictable direction. For predicting outside the observed range, consider whether the polynomial trend is substantively plausible --- does reading ability really follow a cubic curve? The safest approach is to collect new data in the target age range rather than relying on extrapolation. ::: ::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"} ## Exercise 6.7: Discretizing a Continuous Predictor --- What Is Lost? (Conceptual) A common practice in applied research is to split a continuous predictor into categories (e.g., dividing income into "low," "medium," and "high" groups). Compared to keeping income as a continuous predictor (with a polynomial or other smooth specification), what information is lost by discretizing? If two students have incomes of $\$25{,}001$ and $\$24{,}999$ and the "low/medium" cutoff is at $\$25{,}000$, how does the piecewise model treat them differently from the polynomial model? Under what circumstances might discretization still be a reasonable choice? ::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Hint for Exercise 6.7 Discretization throws away within-group variation: all students in the "low" group are treated as having the same predicted outcome, regardless of whether their income is $\$5{,}000$ or $\$24{,}999$. The two students straddling the cutoff are assigned to different groups and receive different predictions despite being nearly identical. A polynomial model would assign them nearly identical predicted values because it uses the actual continuous values. Discretization can be reasonable when there are genuine thresholds in the data (e.g., eligibility cutoffs for programs) or when the relationship is truly flat within groups but jumps between them. Otherwise, it reduces statistical power and can create artificial discontinuities. :::
